rad_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: No nodules requiring follow-up are identified.

Discoid atelectasis in both lungs. Irregular thickening of the walls of some bronchi, with bronchial contents in the lumens (predominantly at the level of both lower lobes). No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass lesions or lymphadenopathy are identified.

Heart: No cardiomegaly is identified. No significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification is identified in the left circumflex and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as mild.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, mild wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Demineralization of bone tissue.

Upper abdomen: Splenic artery aneurysm measures up to 1.2 × 0.9 cm.

Thyroid gland: Thyroid nodules measure up to 5 mm and density up to 127 HU, with fairly well-defined margins.
rad_impression_en:
1. No nodules requiring follow-up per Fleischner Society recommendations. Bronchial contents and bronchial wall thickening in both lungs, which may correspond to bronchitis.

2. Coronary calcification is identified, qualitatively assessed as mild to moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

3. Mild atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

4. Splenic artery aneurysm.

5. Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Demineralization of bone tissue.

6. Thyroid nodules; per "Managing Incidental Thyroid Nodules Detected on Imaging: White Paper of the ACR Incidental Thyroid Findings Committee, 2014", no further follow-up is required.
val_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: A few solid micronodules measure 2 mm in both lungs (series 5, images 78, 153, 164). Discoid atelectasis in the right middle and lower lobes. Bronchial walls are irregularly thickened, with bronchial contents in the lumens (predominantly in both lower lobes), air trapping due to obstructed airways. No pleural effusion. No areas of abnormal contrast enhancement are identified.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No masses, areas of abnormal contrast enhancement, or lymphadenopathy are identified.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. It is impossible to assess the presence of coronary calcium without a non-contrast series.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated. No pulmonary arterial contrast filling defects are identified.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Severe demineralization of bone tissue is present (decreased density of vertebral bodies: T12 - 39 HU, L1 - 38 HU, L2 - 38 HU).

Upper abdomen: Splenic artery aneurysm measuring up to 1.2 cm × 9 mm is identified, unchanged. Signs of nutcracker syndrome are identified with the pre-compression left renal vein measuring 8 mm in diameter and 3 mm at the level of compression (compression ratio 2.6, previously 2.0), along with a reduced aortic-SMA angle of approximately 20 degrees. In this setting, there is dilatation of the left gonadal vein up to 9 mm (previously 8 mm).

Thyroid gland: Multiple nodules measure up to 5 mm in both lobes. ACR White Paper recommendations (Hoang, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2015;12:143-150) suggest the following: No need for further follow-up.
val_impression_en:
1. A few solid micronodules in both lungs. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following:
- For patients with low risk of lung cancer, no need for further follow-up.
- For patients with high risk of lung cancer, generally no need for follow-up. An optional follow-up chest CT at 12 months could be performed if there is high index of suspicion. If unchanged, no further follow-up is necessary.

2. Bronchial contents and bronchial wall thickening in both lungs, which may correspond to bronchitis. Air trapping due to obstructed airways.

3. No pulmonary arterial contrast filling defects are identified. It is impossible to assess the presence of coronary calcium without a non-contrast series.

4. Severe demineralization of bone tissue (osteoporosis).

5. Splenic artery aneurysm, unchanged. ACR White Paper recommendations (Khosa, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2013;10:789-794) suggest the following: Follow-up evaluation with CT or MRI annually.

6. Signs of nutcracker syndrome with left gonadal vein dilatation up to 9 mm. According to the ACR White Paper recommendations (Khosa, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2013;10:789-794) in the absence of hypertension, hematuria, proteinuria, or symptoms of pelvic congestion syndrome, follow-up is generally not required.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

rad_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura:  
In the posterior parts of both lungs, mild reticular changes. In the anterior parts of the left lung and in lung bases of the right lung, discoid atelectasis.  
Along the interlobar pleura bilaterally, perifissural nodules, measuring up to 3 × 4 mm.  
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No masses or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. Pericardial effusion, with maximum thickness up to 8 mm. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.  
Aortic valve calcification.  
Right coronary artery stenting.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. Calcified atherosclerotic plaques in the wall of the thoracic aorta. The main pulmonary artery is dilated up to 30 mm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T11 - 89 HU, T12 - 75 HU, L1 - up to 77 HU. Decreased vertebral body height: T4 up to 26%, T5 by 31%, T6 by 28%, T7 by 28%. Healing fracture of the right 9th rib and the left 2nd, 3rd, 4th, 5th, 6th, and 7th ribs.

Upper abdomen: Unremarkable.
rad_impression_en:
1. No nodules requiring follow-up per Fleischner Society recommendations.

2. Perifissural nodules in both lungs.

3. Pericardial effusion.

4. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Aortic valve calcification.

5. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.0 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

6. Decreased vertebral body density (CT signs of demineralization).

7. Decreased vertebral body height at T4, T5, T6, and T7.

8. Multiple healing rib fractures bilaterally.
val_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: In the dorsal lungs gravitational changes, in the left lingula discoid atelectasis. Along the interlobar pleura bilaterally, perifissural nodules (perifissural lymph nodes) measure up to 4 mm, require no follow-up according to ACR Fleischner Society recommendations. A few scattered micronodules up to 2 mm in both lungs (series 5, slice 78, 95, 97, 102, 179). No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No masses or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: The heart is slightly enlarged due to increased size of the ventricles. Pericardial effusion, with maximum thickness up to 8 mm, increased slightly compared to Abdomen CT on July 1, 2025. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as severe. Aortic and mitral valve calcifications. Metallic foreign body in the right atrium, not identified on Abdomen CT on July 1, 2025.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. Multiple calcified atherosclerotic plaques in the wall of the thoracic aorta. The main pulmonary artery is dilated up to 30 mm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T11 - 89 HU, T12 - 75 HU, L1 - up to 77 HU. Multiple healing fractures of the right 9th rib and the left 2nd, 3rd, 4th, 5th, 6th, 7th, and 8th ribs.

Upper abdomen: Unremarkable.
val_impression_en:
1. A few scattered micronodules up to 2 mm in both lungs. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following:
- For patients with low risk of lung cancer, no need for further follow-up.
- For patients with high risk of lung cancer, generally no need for follow-up. An optional follow-up chest CT at 12 months could be performed if there is high index of suspicion. If unchanged, no further follow-up is necessary.

2. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as severe. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has known CAD, prior CABG/stent, trauma indications or intraoperative CTs.

3. Small-volume pericardial effusion, with minimal interval worsening compared with the prior study.

4. Aortic and mitral valve calcifications. Metallic foreign body in the right atrium, not identified on Abdomen CT on July 1, 2025.

5. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.0 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

6. Decreased vertebral body density (CT signs of demineralization/osteoporosis).

7. Multiple healing rib fractures bilaterally.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

rad_findings_en:
FINDINGS:

Lungs and pleura:
Pulmonary nodules in series 5 are as follows:
Right upper lobe:
- Multiple centrilobular tree-in-bud nodules, images 127-144.
Right middle lobe:
- Multiple centrilobular tree-in-bud nodules, images 90-114.
Right lower lobe:
- A few centrilobular tree-in-bud nodules and ground-glass opacities, images 157-169.
Left lower lobe:
- A few centrilobular tree-in-bud nodules and ground-glass opacities, images 76-87.
- Solid micronodule measuring up to 2 mm, image 119.
These findings are unchanged compared with the CT of the abdomen including the lung bases from April 02, 2026.
Linear atelectasis in lung bases of the left lower lobe.
Parenchymal bands in lung bases of the left upper lobe.
Apical fibrosis on the right and left.
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:
No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart:
No cardiomegaly.
No significant pericardial effusion.
Coronary calcification in the left anterior descending artery, qualitatively assessed as mild.

Great vessels:
The thoracic aorta is not dilated.
The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.
A few calcifications of the aortic arch and descending aorta.
Aortic valve calcification.

Skeletal structures and chest wall:
Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine, increased kyphosis.
Osteoarthritis of the shoulder joints.

Upper abdomen:
- In the left kidney at the mid pole, a large cyst measuring 6.4 cm in diameter, with fluid content, density 4 HU, without septations or a solid component (per noncontrast scanning).
- In the right kidney at the mid pole, large cysts measuring 4.8 cm and 2.4 cm in diameter, with fluid content, density 12 HU, without septations or a solid component (per noncontrast scanning); a renal sinus cyst measuring 3 mm in diameter.
rad_impression_en:
1. Multiple tree-in-bud nodules and ground-glass opacities, consistent with respiratory bronchiolitis, unchanged compared with the CT of the abdomen including the lung bases from April 02, 2026.

2. Solid small pulmonary nodule; considering risk factors, optional follow-up at 12 months per the Updated Fleischner Society guideline 2017.

3. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as mild. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

4. A few large renal cysts, likely benign. No further follow-up is required.

5. Aortic calcification and mild aortic valve calcification.

6. Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with increased kyphosis. Osteoarthritis of the shoulder joints.
val_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura:

Pulmonary nodules in series 5 are as follows:
Right upper lobe:
-Multiple centrilobular tree-in-bud nodules (images 90–114).
Right middle lobe:
-Multiple centrilobular tree-in-bud nodules (images 127–144).
Right lower lobe:
-A few centrilobular tree-in-bud nodules and ground-glass opacities (images 157–169).
Left lower lobe:
-Solid nodule up to 2 mm (image 120).
These findings are unchanged compared with the CT of the abdomen including the lung bases from April 2, 2026.
Linear atelectasis is present in the bilateral lung bases. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification in the left anterior descending artery, qualitatively assessed as mild.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated and is mildly calcified. Mild aortic valve calcification. The pulmonary trunk is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.
Mild S-shaped scoliosis of the thoracic spine with curve apices at the level of T4 and T8.
Moderate degenerative changes of the shoulder joints bilaterally.

Upper abdomen: Please see separately dictated abdomen CT performed concurrently.
val_impression_en:
1. Multiple bilateral tree-in-bud nodules and ground-glass opacities, consistent with respiratory bronchiolitis, unchanged compared with the CT of the abdomen including the lung bases from April 02, 2026.

2. Solid nodule of the left lower lobe.  ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: For patients with low risk of lung cancer, no need for follow-up. For patients with high risk of lung cancer, generally no need for follow-up. An optional chest CT at 12 months could be performed if there is high index of suspicion.

3.  Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as mild.The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has known CAD, prior CABG/stent, trauma indications or intraoperative CTs.

5. Mild aortic calcification. Mild aortic valve calcification.

6. Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

rad_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: Micro-nodules in both lungs, measuring up to 2 mm in diameter. Fibrotic changes in the apices of both lungs with small calcifications within. Reticular changes in the upper lobes of both lungs. Small perifissural areas of increased density along the interlobar pleura bilaterally. Discoid atelectasis and traction bronchiectasis in the left lingular segments.  
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No masses or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No significant pericardial effusion. Coronary artery calcification in the right coronary artery, left main coronary artery, and left anterior descending artery, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Aortic valve calcification. Mitral valve clipping.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated. Calcified atherosclerotic plaques in the wall of the thoracic aorta. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T11 - 82 HU, T12 - 57 HU, L1 - 67 HU, L2 - 122 HU.

Upper abdomen: Left-sided Bochdalek hernia. In segment 5 of the right hepatic lobe, a heterogeneous round lesion with partially calcified walls and well-defined smooth margins, measuring up to 1.4 cm in diameter, density 16 HU. Common bile duct is dilated up to 9 mm.
rad_impression_en:
1. Micro-nodules in the right upper lobe. Fleischner Society recommendations may not be applicable considering the clinical history. Follow-up per oncology follow-up protocol.

2. Post-tuberculous changes in the apices of both lungs.

3. Coronary artery calcification in the right coronary artery, left main coronary artery, and left anterior descending artery, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Aortic valve calcification.

6. Decreased vertebral body density (CT signs of demineralization).

7. Heterogeneous lesion in segment 5 of the right hepatic lobe (per “Management of Incidental Liver Lesions on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee,” 2017, liver MRI is recommended).

8. Choledochoectasia.
val_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: In both lungs, large pleuroapical calcified scarring with lung distortion and pulmonary reticulations, and multiple calcified micronodules. In the right upper lobe, solid nodule measures 4 mm (image 55, series 5). Multiple micronodules measure up to 2 mm, mostly in the interlobar pleura. No consolidation or pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No masses or lymphadenopathy. Small hiatal hernia. 

Heart: Enlarged heart chambers. No significant pericardial effusion. Coronary artery calcification in the right coronary artery, left main coronary artery, and left anterior descending artery, qualitatively assessed as moderate. Aortic valve calcification. Mitral valve clipping.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated. Calcified atherosclerotic plaques in the wall of the thoracic aorta. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T11 - 82 HU, T12 - 57 HU, L1 - 67 HU, L2 - 122 HU (CT signs of demineralization).

Upper abdomen: In segment 5 of the right hepatic lobe, a heterogeneous round lesion with partially calcified walls and well-defined smooth margins, measures up to 1.4 cm in diameter, density 16 HU. Common bile duct is dilated up to 1.1 cm.
val_impression_en:
1. In both lungs pleuroapical calcified scarring and multiple calcified micronodules may represent post-inflammatory changes/healed granulomatous disease. Solid nodule measures 4 mm, multiple micronodules. Fleischner Society recommendations may not be applicable considering the clinical history (history of skin/face cancer). Follow-up per oncology follow-up protocol.

2. Coronary artery calcification in the right coronary artery, left main coronary artery, and left anterior descending artery, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Aortic valve calcification.

3. Decreased vertebral body density (CT signs of demineralization).

4. Hepatic cyst with peripheral focal areas of calcification in segment 5 of the right hepatic lobe, probably benign. ACR White Paper recommendations (Gore, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1429-1437) suggest the following: No need for further follow-up.

5. Dilated bile ducts.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

rad_findings_en:
FINDINGS:

Lungs and pleura: Solid subpleural nodule measuring 1.5 cm in sagittal plane in the supradiaphragmatic portions of the right lower lobe (lung window, image 199/512), unchanged.  
Pulmonary nodules on lung window are as follows, subpleural, triangular and ovoid in shape:  
Right upper lobe:  
- Solid nodule measuring 4 mm on image 32.  
Right middle lobe:  
- Solid nodule measuring 3 mm on image 57 (not previously seen).  
Right upper lobe:  
- Solid nodule measuring 4 mm on image 71 (not previously seen).  
In the left lung, adjacent to the inferior portion of the left major fissure, several micronodules measuring 2-3 mm persist.  
Mild subpleural scar persists in the posteromedial subpleural region of the right lung apex. A few parenchymal bands persist in the anterior portions of the right middle lobe. A few areas of subsegmental atelectasis in the lower lobes of both lungs. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. New pericardial effusion in basal portions with thickness up to 6 mm. Coronary calcification is present in the left anterior descending and first diagonal coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as mild.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. A few calcified atherosclerotic plaques in the walls of the thoracic and abdominal aorta. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Mild degenerative changes of the shoulder joints and spine with sclerosis of the vertebral endplates and articular surfaces, and mild osteophytes.

Upper abdomen: Lesion of the medial crus of the left adrenal gland measuring 1.3 cm (previously up to 1.2 cm), density up to +14 HU. Area of hepatic steatosis in segment 4 (not previously seen). Stable calcifications in the upper portion of the right breast.

Thyroid gland: Hypodense nodule in the right thyroid lobe measuring 1.2 cm, and in the left lobe up to 4 mm.
rad_impression_en:
1. CT appearance of a stable solid nodule in the right lower lobe compared with September 25, 2023. For additional characterization, positron emission tomography/CT may be performed.

2. Several other smaller nodules in both lungs, predominantly in the region of the fissures, including new nodules. Per 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, no additional CT follow-up is recommended for low risk; for high risk, CT follow-up in 12 months is recommended.

3. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as mild.  
The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.  
Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has known CAD, prior CABG/stent, trauma indications or intraoperative CTs.

4. Mild pericardial effusion, not previously seen.

5. Mild atherosclerosis of the thoracic and abdominal aorta.

6. Thyroid nodules. Per American College of Radiology (White Paper) (Hoang, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2015;12:143-150): no further follow-up is required. Not included in the scan range on the prior study.

7. Left adrenal lesion, likely an adenoma. Stable compared with CT from September 25, 2023. Per Management of Incidental Adrenal Masses: A White Paper of the American College of Radiology Incidental Findings Committee, 2017, no further follow-up is required.

8. Mild hepatic steatosis (not previously seen).

9. Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine and shoulder joints.
val_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: In the lung parenchyma, solid nodules are identified, in series 204 presented as follows:
Right upper lobe:  
- Perifissural solid nodule measures 3 mm (image 64), unchanged, requires no follow-up.
Right middle lobe:  
- Solid nodule measures 3 mm on image 114 (not previously seen).  
Right lower lobe:
- Subpleural solid nodule measures 1.4 × 1.2 cm in the supradiaphragmatic portions (image 172). No change in size is observed compared to CT on September 25, 2023, September 9, 2022, however, an increase in density and a change in shape (more flattened) are observed.
Left lung:
- Multiple perifissural nodules up to 4 mm (lymph nodes), unchanged, require no follow-up.
Mild subpleural scar persists in the posteromedial subpleural region of the right lung apex and in the anterior portions of the right middle lobe, unchanged. A few subsegmental atelectasis in the lower lobes of both lungs. No pleural effusion. Mild bronchial wall thickening, a small amount of mucus in the trachea.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. Pericardial effusion with thickness up to 6 mm, previously not seen. Coronary calcifications are present in the left anterior descending and left circumflex arteries, qualitatively assessed as mild.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. A few calcified atherosclerotic plaques in the walls of the thoracic and abdominal aorta. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Bone islands of the right humeral head, 5th and 6th ribs on the left.

Upper abdomen: Lesion of the medial crus of the left adrenal gland measures 1.2 cm (unchanged), density up to +8 HU. Area of hepatic steatosis in segment 4 (previously not seen).

Thyroid gland: Two hypodense nodules in the right thyroid lobe measure 1.5 cm and 7 mm; on the Chest CT on September 9, 2022, they measure 1.3 cm and 4 mm, respectively. Heterogeneous structure of the left lobe.
val_impression_en:
1. Subpleural right lower lobe pulmonary nodule measures 1.4 × 1.2 cm, stable in size since September 9, 2022, with interval change in density and shape, probably benign. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: No need for further follow-up for patients with high or low risk of lung cancer. 
New solid nodule in the right middle lobe measures 3 mm. According to ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) no additional CT follow-up is recommended for low risk; for high risk, CT follow-up in 12 months is optional.

2. Mild bronchial wall thickening, a small amount of mucus in the trachea may represent bronchitis .

3. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as mild. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has known CAD, prior CABG/stent, trauma indications or intraoperative CTs.

4. Small-volume pericardial effusion, not previously seen.

5. Thyroid nodules with increase in size. Further evaluation with thyroid ultrasound recommended if not already obtained elsewhere.

6. Left adrenal lesion, likely an adenoma. Stable compared with CT from September 25, 2023. Per Management of Incidental Adrenal Masses: A White Paper of the American College of Radiology Incidental Findings Committee, 2017, no further follow-up is required.

7. Focal hepatic steatosis (not previously seen).

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
